content: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIOTEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS A.V. Kovalenko1, M. Wessling2,3, V.V. Nikonenko1, S.A. Mareev1, I.A. Moroz1, E. Evdochenko1,2, M.Kh. Urtenov1 1Kuban State University, Membrane Institute, Stavropolskaya Str. 149, 350040, Krasnodar, Russian Federation 2RWTH Aachen University, Chemical Process Engineering, Forckenbeckstrasse 51, 52074, Aachen, Germany 3DWI - Leibniz Institute for Interactive Materials, Forckenbeckstrasse 50, 52074, Aachen, Germany

Abstract Overlimiting current regimes are of practical interest for electrodialysis with ion-exchange membranes (IEMs) operating at current densities significantly higher than the limiting current density. Such high ion currents across the membranes allows reducing the investments into expensive IEMs and stacks. Electroconvection (EC) is the major contributor to the overlimiting current and concepts to utilize EC are highly desired. Most of the known theoretical works describing EC in electrodialysis channels are performed when only a single cation exchange membrane is considered. The presence of a neighboring anion-exchange membrane has not been considered so far. We report the results of direct numerical simulations of ion and water transport involving EC in an electrodialysis channel formed by a cationand an anion-exchange membrane. A 2D “basic” model involving the Nernst-Planck-Poisson-Navier-Stokes equations is introduced. Details of spatio-temporal changes in the distribution of concentrations and space charge patterns as well as the interactions of EC vortices are analyzed. The presence of the anion-exchange membrane in the transition to a close-to-chaotic regime at high potential difference is examined. For the first time, we discover the phenomenon of space-charge breakdown where the space charges of opposite sign formed at the two membranes are short-circuited throughout the desalination channel.

f space-charge breakdown where the space charges of opposite sign formed at the two membranes are short-circuited throughout the desalination channel. The details of the mechanism of this phenomenon are analyzed using the 2D model and a simplified 1D model. Simulations show that the space-charge breakdown leads to a decrease in the size and number of EC vortices in the region of breakdown, which results in a reduction of the local current density. This phenomenon, apparently, determines the upper limit of the possible increase in the mass transfer rate by electroconvection. Keywords: Ion-exchange membranes; Electrodialysis; Concentration polarization; Overlimiting current; Electroconvection

1. Introduction Electrodialysis (ED) is a widely used electrically driven membrane process for solution desalination, concentration and separation of ions [1, 2]. The main economic advantage of this technique is that during ED ions are removed from the feed solution. In pressure-driven ion separation and concentration processes, the solvent water passes through the membrane. Therefore, the costs of ED process decrease approximately proportionally to the decrease in ion concentration, while in reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF) these costs only slightly depend on solution concentration [3]. Consequently, ED is a significantly less energy consuming process than RO and NF, when the feed solution concentration is sufficiently low [3, 4]. Recent estimations made by Patel, Biesheuvel and Elimelech [5] show that the critical value of total dissolved solids in the feed solution is 3 g L–1. Another advantage is the ability to obtain highly concentrated solutions and high rates of water recovery, which is not possible in the case of using only RO and NF [6, 7]. With a process similar to ED using capacitive flow electrodes, one can even realize deep desalination and high concentration of solutions [8,9].

, 7]. With a process similar to ED using capacitive flow electrodes, one can even realize deep desalination and high concentration of solutions [8,9]. However, when treating feed solution towards deep desalination, there is a serious limitation of ED: ionic fluxes through the membranes do not follow the driving force but are limited by the diffusion from the bulk electrolyte through laminar boundary layers to the membrane/electrolyte solution interface, wellknown as concentration polarization [10]. This leads to a significant increment of electrical resistance in the diluate compartment, which results in an increase in the ratio of the energy consumption to the minimum required by thermodynamics [5]. To overcome this challenge, narrow ED diluate channels [11], ion-exchange resin fillers [12] and ion-conductive spacers within diluate channels (electrodeionisation) [10] and other methods [13] are used. A phenomenon, that various authors designate as a possible means of overcoming this limitation, is electroconvection [14–17]. Electroconvection (EC) is generally understood as a current-induced volume transport, when an electric field is imposed through the charged solution [18]. This volume transport gives rise to microvortices at the depleted membrane/electrolyte solution interface. The vortices mix the solution near the surface and bring fresh concentrated solution from the bulk solution and delivers it to the membrane surface.

. The vortices mix the solution near the surface and bring fresh concentrated solution from the bulk solution and delivers it to the membrane surface. Simultaneously, it reinjects the depleted solution from the membrane surface to the bulk [19–21]. Unstable electroconvection producing the vortices with the size comparable with the diffusion layer thickness has been first mathematically predicted in 2000 [14], and later experimentally verified in 2008 [22]. More details of the chaotic turbulence even at zero Reynolds number have been quantified recently through systematic computational visualization experiments [23]. EC increases mass transfer rate even when one operates in conventional ED stack configuration. Experiment shows [15] that due to reducing the area of expensive ion-exchange membranes, it is economically advantageous to apply overlimiting currents (OLC) when treating dilute solutions. Over the last ten years, various new configurations of ED stacks where EC is effectively used for enhancing mass transfer rate have been proposed and intensively discussed. These new systems include ‘ion concentration polarization (ICP) desalination’, which uses cationexchange membranes and microporous membranes as splitters [11, 19], ‘shock ED’ [25–30], applying also cation-exchange membranes but with a porous material filling the space between them. Other microelectrodialysis [31] and microfluidic [32–35] devices are described.

e membranes but with a porous material filling the space between them. Other microelectrodialysis [31] and microfluidic [32–35] devices are described. The latter uses the EC phenomenon for electrophoresis, in particular for DNA sequencing [36], electroosmotic pumps [37–39] and others [40–42]. This introduction shows that the use of EC is considered as one of the possible routes to improve large-scale electrodialysis as well as nano-microfluidic processes. Its origin, the complex interplay of dynamic ion concentration gradients with space charge gradients causes volumetric forces moving the liquid in chaotic patterns depending on the potential difference over the fluid to be deionized. In this paper, we answer the question whether the spatio-temporal space charges at the membrane–fluid interface may interact or even eliminate each other when oppositely charged ion exchange membranes operated in close vicinity and when opposite space-charges interact with each other in time and space.

2. Background Electromembrane mass transport processes involving EC are mathematically complex since the local deviation from electrical neutrality and coupling between ion and water transport must be taken into account. Modern theories of electrodialysis are based on a two-dimensional model by Sonin and Probstein [43,44], named by the authors hydrodynamic theory of ED, known also as convectiondiffusion model [45].

According to this model, when an electrolyte solution flows between an AEM and a CEM in the absence of a spacer, a concentration profile with a clearly pronounced plateau in the center of the channel and diffusion layers at the membrane surface are established at a small distance from the channel entrance. It is the “developing” region of the channel (Fig. 1, line 2). At a sufficiently large distance, in the “developed” region, the diffusion layers overlap and fill the entire channel (Fig. 1, curves 3 and 4). The concentration profile is not symmetric for the electrolytes containing cation and anion with different mobility in solution (as in the case of NaCl considered in Fig. 1). Since the mobility of the Cl- ion is higher than that of Na+, the current density, which is limiting for Na+, is underlimiting for Cl-. At this current density, the electrolyte concentration reaches a close to zero value at the CEM, but not at the AEM. The most interesting is the “developing” region with a small thickness of diffusion layers (); the use of spacers [3,46–48] or membranes with profiled [49] and patterned surface [17,50–52] allows enhancing mass transfer and reducing the effective value of . In the practice of ED, only this “developing” region occurs because the presence of spacers imposes periodic disturbance of the developing flow. As for the shape of concentration profile, experiments indeed show (Fig.

the presence of spacers imposes periodic disturbance of the developing flow. As for the shape of concentration profile, experiments indeed show (Fig. 2a) that when a relatively small voltage/current density is applied in a spacer-free feed channel, the concentration profiles characteristic to the “developing” region are observed. A diffusion layer with a total thickness δtot, where the concentration changes occur, is clearly identified (Fig. 2a). This layer includes the Nernst diffusion boundary layer of thickness δN, which determines the concentration gradient at the membrane surface and the value of ion flux through the membrane, in particular, that of the limiting current density [53]. δN is the thickness of the zone where diffusion is the ratedetermining mechanism of ion transfer. However, in the overlimiting current regime, the distribution of concentrations differs significantly from the classical theory [43]. The emergence of EC vortices at i>ilim leads to flattering the concentration profile near the membrane surface (Fig. 2b): an electroconvection-dominated zone of thickness d appears within the diffusion layer. With that, the diffusion-dominated zone is shifted away from the membrane/solution boundary; the thickness δ'N of this zone decreases with increasing current density, while δtot increases [21].

shifted away from the membrane/solution boundary; the thickness δ'N of this zone decreases with increasing current density, while δtot increases [21]. When the current is many times higher than the limiting one, and the voltage is of the order of 10 V per cell pair, a near-chaotic state occurs: the concentration distribution becomes non-monotonous, enriched and depleted regions alternate in the channel space (Fig. 2c).

There are a number of theoretical publications describing overlimiting current electrodialysis. Most of them use a 2D mathematical description applying the Nernst-PlanckPoisson and Navier-Stokes equations. At relatively low voltages, electroconvection occurs as electroosmosis of the first kind [54–57] or (quasi-)equilibrium electroosmosis [58–60]. In this case, the solution near the membrane surface comes into motion under the action of the applied electric field on the (quasi-)equilibrium space charge region (SCR) (electrical double layer, EDL). The latter exists even in the absence of the external electric field and only slightly depends on the applied current/voltage. In this mode of EC, no visible vortices are visualized, and no significant effect on mass transfer is expected. However, when the current density exceeds its limiting value and an extended SCR appears, the thickness of which is much larger than that of the EDL, significant vortices emerge. They can essentially increase the mass transfer rate, as it was first theoretically predicted by Rubinstein and Zaltzman [14]. This EC mode is named electroosmosis of the second kind [54,55] or non-equilibrium electroosmosis [14,56,58]. The dynamics of formation of EC vortices has been extensively studied [61,62].

f the second kind [54,55] or non-equilibrium electroosmosis [14,56,58]. The dynamics of formation of EC vortices has been extensively studied [61,62]. While in the absence of forced flow, the vortices are paired and have a close size [14,63], the forced flow reduces the size of the vortices rotating so that their tangential velocity at a relatively large distance from the membrane is opposite to that of the shear flow. The vortices can be drawn by the tangential shear flow. The formation of vortex clusters, in which paired vortices rotate in opposite directions with different velocities, was described by Urtenov et al. [64]. Mani and co-workers [17,20] studied the transition of EC vortex structure from regular coherent individual vortices to chaotic multiscale patterns, which are accompanied by current oscillations. Demekhin and co-workers [65,66] found that overlimiting current regimes have supercritical and subcritical states; these states are mainly determined by the hydrodynamic conditions and voltage. Druzgalski et al. [67] found that the surface-averaged concentration profile has a well distinguished flattened zone, which proves that EC mixing of the depletion zone is the main trend. The similar peculiarity of concentration profile was emphasized also in Refs. [21,61]. Roghmans et al. [52] show that the direction of the vortices can be even induced by the surface potential. The role of membrane surface heterogeneity was examined Wessling et al. [68] and in Refs. [69,70].

tices can be even induced by the surface potential. The role of membrane surface heterogeneity was examined Wessling et al. [68] and in Refs. [69,70]. It was found that the mass transfer rate is higher, if a part of the membrane surface is screened; this electric heterogeneity initiates greater vortices enhancing mass transfer. As it was shown by Liu et al. [71], at very high voltages, collision of the EC vortices causes the overlapping of EC vortices, formed at anionand cationexchange membranes. As a result, an extreme depletion zone appears at the channel outlet. This zone then propagates towards the entrance, similar to the propagation of depletion zone in shock ED [25]. 3D modelling gives additional important information. The vortex pattern in shear flow in a 3D ED channel is helical rather than planar [72]; their shape allows a more effective mixing of solution. Druzgalski and Mani [20] presented an analysis of highly chaotic electroconvective flows using 3D simulation data. In particular, they showed formation of instantaneous mushroom-like plumes of ion-depleted fluid. Kang and Kwak described the laboratory observation of three- dimensional electroconvection on a permselective surface [73]. By combining experiment and scaling analysis, they successfully distinguished three 3D patterns (polygonal, transverse, or longitudinal rolls) according to electric Rayleigh, Reynolds and Schmidt numbers. Experimental 3D visualization has not been performed so far.

The main emphasis in the cited works was placed on the role of vortex structures in the transition to a state considered as chaotic or close to chaotic. However, the role of the space-charge region (SCR) and changes in its structure during such a transition have not been examined. There are only a few papers [62,64,71], where the role of both anionand cation-exchange membrane in the formation of vortex patterns was considered. Next to the spatio-temporal ion concentration gradients bridging the channel width, we are interested in the stability of the SCR at either of the two membranes. We are particularly addressing the questions (a) whether space charge breakdown exists and (b) whether it interferes with the local flow and ion gradients.

3. 1D Model 3.1. Model formulation To address the above questions, we will consider two mathematical models, a 1D and a 2D model, accompanied by new experimental visualization of the spatio-temporal patterns of concentration distribution in a membrane system in an intensive current regime. Using the 1D model, we consider a motionless solution between two membranes and indicate the space-charge breakdown phenomenon as the mutual annihilation of two oppositely charged SCRs, which are formed at the CEM and AEM. With the 2D model, we examine how the emergence of the vortices and the forced convection affect the breakdown phenomenon, and inversely, how the breakdown influences the vortex structures. First, we introduce a 1D model for instructional reasons and to establish the observation of potential space charge breakdown in a hypothetical 1D geometry. In this model, an immobile electrolyte solution is considered between an AEM (on the left) and CEM (on the right) (Fig. 1). The system can be interpreted as a desalination channel, which is closed to the inlet and outlet. A (conduction) electric current of density IF flows from the left to the right. The model involves the material balance equation (1), the Nernst-Planck (2) and Poisson (3) equations, as well as the equation expressing IF as the sum of the contributions from the individual ion flux densities (4):

3.3. Space charge breakdown in 1D system The maximum point on the profile of the space charge density modulus (||) appears, when the current density rises above its limiting value for a given membrane and an extended zone of the SCR emerges. In the case of NaCl, a local maximum of || at the CEM appears at ||=0.87 V; at the AEM, this maximum emerges at ||1.0 V (Fig. 4f). With increasing time (and the potential difference), the local maxima move away from the “native” membranes towards the opposite membrane. These extended SCRs behave like two symmetrical solitary waves (or solitons [74,79]. During the potential sweep, the positions of the maxima of the space charge densities formed at the CEM and AEM are reproached. When the distance between the edges of the positive and negative SCRs becomes sufficiently short, they begin to attract each other, and the speed of their approach increases. When touching each other, they “annihilate”, and the space charge breakdown occurs. From the moment of touching to complete “annihilation”, when the SCRs vanish at both membranes, about 1 second passes.

Fig. 5 shows the time dependence of the conduction and charging current densities near the AEM (at x=0.02H), in the channel center (x=0.5H) and near the CEM (at x=0.98H). The difference between IF at different points is negligible, however, IC in the channel center at t close to 60 s ( close to 600 mV) is nearly zero, while it is significant next to the membranes, since the SCRs are formed there up to approximately 30 s (Fig. 5). Then the charge of SCRs slowly decreases due to the decrease in the electrolyte concentration. IF initially increases, since the potential difference increases with time. However, starting from about 10 s, IF decreases as the electrolyte concentration and, consequently, the solution conductivity reduces. When the waves of SCRs reach the channel center, IC at x=0.5H first increases sharply, then decreases even faster (during 1 s): the space-charge breakdown occurs at t86 s. However this 1D Model indicates the possible existence of a charge breakdown and motivates to expand the modelling into the 2D space with flow through a feed channel between two oppositely charged membranes.

The 2D model used in this study corresponds to the model features described in detail in Ref. [64]. A salt solution flows between anion-exchange and cation-exchange membranes arranged in the same way as in Fig. 1, Fig. 6. A set of the Nernst-Planck-Poisson and NavierStokes coupled equations is used. The formation of the SCR and the EC vortices is of particular interest for this 2D model. Can the space charge breakdown be observed as in the case of the simplified 1D model? How does the presence of vortex structures affect a potential space charge breakdown phenomenon (the electric current chooses low-resistance pathways in the space, similar as in the case of lightning)? Or vice versa, how does the breakdown affect the vortex structures? Does the degree of chaotic flow motion decrease? Are the sizes and number of EC vortices affected?

The current-voltage characteristic (CVC) of the membrane system in the case of NaCl solution is computed using the described above 2D model (Fig. 7). The CVC can be divided in 6 different regions according to the features in system behavior, in particular, depending on the vortex flow patterns. In the first section, where  varies from 0 to approximately 0.1 V, a close to linear increase in the current density occurs. Although the total potential difference includes potential drops of the diffusion / concentration potential at interphase boundaries) [83], this region is often called “ohmic” because the membrane system behaves like a resistor governed by Ohm’s law. The second section is the (inclined) plateau of limiting current (from about 0.2 V to 0.65 V).

Ion transport by diffusion attains its maximum at about 0.2 V and the CVC slope in this region is due to the fact that the local limiting current density is reached non-simultaneously on the entire surface of the membrane. First, the current reaches its limiting value at the end of the channel, where the diffusion layer thickness is the largest and the bulk concentration is the least. When the voltage increases, the local current density does not grow at the channel edge, y=L, but it increases at y<L. With increasing voltage, the part of the channel where the current density has already reached its limit value, is increasing. Accordingly, the current through the entire channel increases. Upon reaching  = 0.625 V, the current density reaches its limiting value almost along the entire membrane length, except for the small input region, y0, where, according to the boundary condition at y = 0, the concentration is equal to C0 for all x. A possible competing mechanism of current increase in the inclined plateau of limiting current can be electroosmosis of the first kind [54–57] (equilibrium electroosmosis [59,60]). Electroconvection of such nature under these conditions would be due to the quasi-equilibrium space charge region (EDL) only, the thickness of which is of the order of the Debye length, about 100 nm as estimated in Section 2.2.1. Curve (2) in Fig. 7 shows the CVC calculated in conditions where EC does not occur.

4.3. Space-charge breakdown in 2D system Figs. 10b and 10c show the level curves and surface of the (С1-С2) function between the AEM and CEM in a desalination channel. This function is the space charge density divided by F. A hill (circled with a red line), representing a local maximum of the space charge density, is seen in the middle of the channel, closer to the AEM. This maximum originates from the maximum of the extended SCR at the CEM, well seen in Fig. 4a presenting the results of the 1D model. As in the 1D model (section 2.1), the maximum moves from the CEM to the AEM when time/voltage increases. At the AEM, there is a local minimum of the negative space charge density. However, in the case of NaCl its absolute value is significantly lower than that at the CEM due to the fact that ilim through the AEM is about 1.5 times greater than that through the CEM. Therefore, we observe the transition of the positive space charge from the CEM membrane to the AEM. The negative space charge formed at the AEM moves also from this membrane towards the CEM (as can be seen in Figs. 4e-4g), however, it is not seen in Figs. 10 and 11 as the near-surface solution regions are not shown because of a high difference in the concentration values in these regions and in the middle of the channel. When two space charge regions meet, one positively charged and the other negatively charged, they break down.

With increasing time, in the 2D desalination channel, like in the case of 1D system, the voltage increases while the concentration decreases. Both changes lead to increasing the distance of the concentration of counterion extremums from the corresponding membrane. Therefore, with increasing time, the two extremums of space charge density, the positive one and the negative one, move towards each other, as in the case of 1D process. The difference is in the presence of the forced convection and electroconvection in the 2D channel. Both kinds of convection, especially their interactions, add a factor of randomness, which is amplified by the instability of the SCR with an extremum of space charge density in the bulk of the ED channel. Figs. 12a and 12b describe the evolution of the space charge density and the normal component of the current density in the time interval involving the discharge phenomenon occurring between 594.5 s and 595 s at y1450 m. Figs. 13a-13c show the change in the fluid streamlines at the same time interval. The red circle shows approximatively the region of the discharge.

During space-charge breakdown, the space charge absolute values in the channel bulk (outside of the quasi-equilibrium regions of the EDL) decreases (Fig. 12a), like in the case of 1D a b c a b model (Fig. 4). This leads to decreasing the driving force for electroconvection. As a consequence, the EC vortices become reduced in the region where the “annihilation” takes place, and the local current density across the membrane surface in the vicinity of this region decreases (like as in 1D model, Fig. 5a). The state just before the discharge is shown in Fig. 10. A red band (Fig. 10b) presenting the elevated positive charge density extends from the CEM (at y1700 m) to the AEM (at y1400 m). The discharge occurs at the AEM between 594.5 s and 595 s at y between 1400 and 1500 m. Fig. 12a shows that at 595 s there is a local maximum of the space charge density at a distance x100 m from the AEM in a section y=1450 m; at 595.5 s this maximum disappears. The normal component of the current density, Ix, in the section y=1450 m, decreases gradually starting at 594 s and reaching the minimum at 595 s when the discharge occurs (Fig. 12b). A vortex, initially presented in the vicinity of the discharge, shifts towards the CEM (Figs. 13a and 13b), which should cause the decrease in Ix at y=1450 m.

However, soon after the discharge, at 595.5 s, there is a sharp increase in Ix (Fig. 12 b). Although at y=1450 m and 60<x<350 (m), the space charge density is nearly zero, the increase in current density (Fig. 12b) can be due to a newly appeared vortex at the AEM with the center at y1350 m (Fig. 13c). As the above consideration shows, relatively high values of the space charges formed near the oppositely charged membranes and a small distance between the membranes create conditions for their “annihilation”: the convergence of the centers of these oppositely charged space charges invariably ends with their mutual elimination. As a consequence, the rate of electroconvection and mass transfer in the vicinity of space-charge breakdown decreases. Although the current density can locally increase in the section where the charge breakdown occurred, this phenomenon is repeated in another section. Therefore, the phenomenon of space-charge breakdown determines the upper limit of the increase in the mass transfer rate due to electroconvection.

4.4. Concentration and space-charge density patterns At high voltages and low concentrations, the patterns of concentration and space charge fields are rather complicated. The regions of high and low concentration, high and low charge density alternate in the 2D channel. Moreover, one can see that there are bands of high and low space charge density, which stretch from one membrane to another and which alternate in the longitudinal direction (Fig. 11). Space charge breakdown occurs along the routes of relatively high conductivity, not necessarily normal to the membrane surface (Fig. 11). There are similarities to lightning, that is the electrostatic discharge of two electrically charged regions in the atmosphere or ground: the charge of these two regions must be great enough, while the distance between them small; the discharge passes through domains of relatively high conductivity. However, the duration of lightning is very fast, while a space-charge breakdown lasts about 1 s, the time is controlled by ion electrodiffusion at a distance of about 1/10 of the distance between the membranes (Fig. 4g); the lightning discharge current is very high, while during space-charge breakdown in membrane system, there is no increase in the value of conductance current (Fig. 5a), but a splash in the charging current is observed (Fig. 5b). In both cases, space-charge breakdown ends with a decrease in current density (Figs.

5a), but a splash in the charging current is observed (Fig. 5b). In both cases, space-charge breakdown ends with a decrease in current density (Figs. 5a, b) and abatement of coupled effects (such as electroconvection in membrane channel, Fig. 13). The computation shows that negatively charged regions emerge near the CEM at sufficiently high voltages, regions 5 and 6 in CVC, Figs. 9 b,c and Figs. 10 b,c. Since the surface of a CEM is charged negatively, this is a rather unexpected effect. In a sense, it is the reverse of the space charge breakdown. This effect is apparently caused by the local action of several factors: electromigration, diffusion and convection (forced and electroconvection) in conditions of a strong external electric field. Although this effect is absent in the case of an immobile solution (the 1D model, Fig. 4), it is difficult to imagine that it can be caused only by the interactions of EC vortices. The interactions between EC vortices and forced convection seems to be important. A certain support for this can be the results of visualization of the vortex patterns in the ED channel with a forced flow (Fig. 13). Space-charge breakdown in the experimentally studied system is not possible, since both membranes forming the considered channel are anion-exchange membranes. In addition, the distance between the membranes and the concentration of the feed solution are too great. According to calculations, at a feed solution concentration of 10-5 mol L-1, an intermembrane distance of 500 μm, and a solution flow rate of 0.1 mm s-1, the breakdowns begin at a voltage of 1.74 V.

At present, computational difficulties do not allow us to say how an increase in the concentration of the feed solution will affect the occurrence of the space-charge breakdown phenomenon. In real systems, it is difficult to detect such a phenomenon in the concentration range of the order of 10-5 mol L-1, as used in calculations, due to the experimental difficulties in realizing a reproducible quasi-stationary process of ED desalination of a very dilute solution. The development of the scenario described by the idealized model applied here can be hindered by side effects: ion exchange between the solution and the membrane, as well as generation of H+ and OH- ions (water splitting) at the solution / membrane boundaries. The impact of these effects on the space-charge breakdown requires additional research.

5. Conclusion Numerical simulation of the ED of solution desalting in the channels formed by anion and cation exchange membranes is reported. During the electrodialysis of dilute solutions at sufficiently high voltages and low rates of forced solution flow, a phenomenon of space-charge breakdown through the solution in the desalting chamber emerges. Under the above conditions, a negative space charge forms near the anion-exchange membrane, and a positive one near the cation-exchange membrane. The curves of the distribution of both negative and positive space charge density in the intermembrane solution have characteristic extrema at some distance from the membrane surface. With an increase in the voltage and a decrease in concentration, these extrema, initially formed near the membrane’s surfaces, approach each other. Ultimately, this ends with a discharge of two space charges of opposite signs. The moment of breakdown is preceded by a high degree of instability of the EC vortex motion, which leads to a high degree of randomness in the distribution of concentration, solution flow rates, and space charge density. Immediately after breakdown, the space charge density near both membranes drops to almost zero locally along the breakdown line. Accordingly, this leads to a sharp decrease in the EC vortex motion in the region of discharge and to a decrease in the local current density.

n line. Accordingly, this leads to a sharp decrease in the EC vortex motion in the region of discharge and to a decrease in the local current density. The breakdown phenomenon described in this theoretical work is a possible scenario for the development of the ED process of desalination at high voltages and low salt concentrations. The observation of such a scenario in real systems can be overshadowed by other simultaneously occurring phenomena: ion exchange between the solution and the membrane, and water splitting at the solution / membrane interface. Taking into account the influence of these effects on the process of ED desalination in intensive current regimes represents yet another herculian effort in unravelling the physical details of overlimiting currents.
